sentences.text: Good morning, Carol.
My first name is Morris, M-O-R-R-I-S, which means Moshe.
Second name is Venezia, V-E-N-E-Z-- as a zebra-- I-A.
My name at birth was [?
Maurice, ?] which is something like Moshe in Hebrew.
I am born in February 1921 in Thessaloniki.
Where was?
The country Thessaloniki is the capital of Macedonia, Greece.
Today, I'm 75 years old.
Well, the way they explain it to me is from the Inquisition of Spain.
At the time, many Jews were there, were living in Spain.
After the Inquisition, they went all over Europe.
And I think, my parents they stopped right in Italy in Venice.
From Spain to Venice, Italy.
And from Italy, they went to Thessaloniki, Greece.
And everybody calling them the Venezianos, Venezianos.
Because they came from Venezia.
And I think this is the only way they got the name Venezia.
The name of my parents was Isaac, my father.
And my mother, they used to call her Dudune in Turkish, which means something beautiful.
Because she was the only-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the only female in the family after four brothers.
What was here making name?
Oh.
Her name from one was Angel.
Not that I know.
But I think was Daisy.
I had one brother, one brother, and three sisters.
My older sister is Rachel, which is Ra-hel.
The other one, which lives now in Italy, is Shlomo or Solomon.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The other girl was Marika.
And the little one was Marta
I was-- I was the oldest.
My father occupation was barber.
My mother never worked.
Well, we were in my neighborhood almost was call them the Baron Hirsch, all the Jewish people living over there.
All poor people, but with wonderful life.
[CRYING]
[CRYING] We had our own house in the neighborhood, yes.
The what?
To us, it was beautiful.
Because we're born over there with two rooms, kitchen, living room, and a big lot in front of the house.
Which later, we sold our house.
And my father built up a two story house in that lot.
So there is there.
We were living very beautiful.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] were happy.
[CRYING]
My Jewish life?
Well, actually my grandfather, I remember, he was at that time around 60, 65 years old.
He never was so religious at that time.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was living with them, my father, my grandfather, and my grandmother.
And he used to wake me up every morning around 6:00 to take me to the temple.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Which, that was until I became 13.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, I got me-- and I got a bar mitzvah.
After that, I say, maybe six months, something like that, they sent me in Italy.
Because my father died at the time.
And I was the older.
My mother was with four kids.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Italian consulate, because we were Italian citizens, even if you born, everybody born in Greece, we were Italian citizen.
And they send me in Italy, in Milan, to a college.
My-- their name, my grandfather name, was like my name.
I took after him.
It was more Morris.
My grandmother, she-- they used to call her Dudune, too.
Because she was beautiful [INAUDIBLE].
In Turkish, Dudune means something good.
I don't know what it means, but something very nice.
Because they loved me so much.
My father passed away.
And I had his name, my grandfather name.
And they took very good care of me.
My father died in 1934.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
No.
I don't remember exactly what kind of [INAUDIBLE] he had.
But I think it was a gallbladder, something.
He had a big operation.
And a couple of days after the operation, he passed away.
In the beginning, till the second elementary, I was in a neighborhood, the Jewish school in [INAUDIBLE].
And after that, because I was Italian citizen, they sent me to an Italian school till third and fourth elementary school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after that-- the name of the school was called [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After the fifth-- elementary fifth, I went to a brand new school they build-- the Italians over there-- called an [NON-ENGLISH], which was not elementary.
It was the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after the elementary, whatever it is.
A-- a high school or gymnasium?
I don't remember that.
And after that, I was till the first [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] class of the-- of the gymnasium or the high school.
And after that, they sent me in Italy.
What were the different, what, of Italian citizen?
Was nothing different.
Because it's not like the United States, anybody who-- who born in the United States, they get right away the United States citizenship.
But in other countries, they don't get it like that.
They still have their citizenship of their parents.
So if I wanted some day to become a Greek citizen, it was when I was going to be 21 years old and to decide if I want to go to serve in the Greek army or to the Italian army.
Which, this never came up.
Because we didn't have a chance, because of the war.
So in other words, your parents were Italian citizens--
Right.
Right.
All their kids, Italian citizens.
Oh, yes.
Of course.
That's why my grandfather was waking up.
And I hated that.
Because wintertime, too cold to wake up at 6 o'clock in the morning to take me to the temple.
After, I got the bar mitzvah.
And I learned everything, which I don't know nothing about it now.
I forgot everything.
Well, something that I remember was that when I put the tefillin in my arm, this is what I remember.
My mother was very religious.
And we had all the Jewish holidays, especially Passover.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We used to read the Haggadah every year-- [INAUDIBLE] year-- every year.
We were a family, very close family with too much love.
[CRYING] [SOBBING] Excuse me, Carol.
I get too-- too emotional when I remember that.
[CRYING] That's why I didn't want-- I never wanted to testify, anyway.
Excuse me.
Go ahead.
No, it was all mixed.
Nice, very nice.
No problem.
The only thing when in class when we had the Christian hour, the Jewish people would go out.
And that's all.
Well, all over the world there's anti-Semitism.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] To me, it's because they're jealous of Jewish people, nothing else.
But with our friends, we didn't have such a thing.
But even at that time, the old people in Greece, they had something against the Jewish people.
Because they were not educated people at the time, but not now, anymore.
Always something funny.
Since I was a boy, I was doing all my own toys by myself.
Because we didn't have no toys at that time.
It's not like now.
And I used to do [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] many, many things.
Even, I had in my mind to do a bicycle, which I never did.
Because it was too expensive at that time.
And nobody had the bicycle.
But I was doing-- I was enjoying all the time to do something with my hands.
Oh, for example, I was doing what they call the thing to shoot for the birds.
Yeah.
Slingshot.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was doing-- I was doing a couple pieces of wood with the steps on it and to go on it and-- and walk with it.
Right, right, exactly.
And excuse my English, [INAUDIBLE].
Because it cannot be like my native language.
I can explain myself in my native language.
But I think I'm doing all right.
So I made many, many other toys.
I can name you thousands of them, many, many others.
Well, I was playing with them.
I was playing with them, yeah.
I didn't have a change to be very close with my brother.
Because when I was 13, they send me in Italy, like I said before.
And when I came back, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a couple girlfriends, which I didn't want my brother-- he's two years younger than I am at that time.
I had my own friends.
And he had his own friends.
And I never liked my brother to come with me to go with a girlfriends.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
No differently at all.
We were very, very lovely family.
We loved each other.
Even some time for dinner, if somebody was missing of us, we had to wait for that missing to come and sit down at the table.
If not, we won't eat.
It was all kosher.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Before Passover, that week, they had to redo it all the kitchenwares.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And to clean all the house nobody [INAUDIBLE] in the house.
Carry the matzohs in a special container.
And they used to do over there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fish, the carp, at the time.
Because I think it was at the time the carp was big enough and very, very delicious.
And then they used to-- to do meatballs of spinach or [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some other kinds, many, many, many other kinds.
But was everything else Passover-- kosher.
We were not kosher at home.
But at that time, everything was kosher.
Everything my mother cooked I still remember.
Like I said before, there were poor people, very poor people.
Because they were living in their community, the Jewish community homes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they used to call them Baron Hirsch.
They were worse than the ghetto after that.
The Germans made a gate over there.
From there they were [INAUDIBLE], because was right at the train station, that neighborhood.
But they had everybody-- they used to have a very good time, especially when the girls were getting married, big parties.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When the-- when the children was born, very good parties.
Poor, but a lot of fun.
A large what?
Oh, yes.
Was a brand new, almost brand new.
A very big one, that was in Baron Hirsch only.
Because we had some other synagogues all over Thessaloniki.
The way I know is a kind of a Baron Hirsch.
Name was a person, a rich person, called him Baron Hirsch.
And because this Baron he helped so much for this neighborhood and for the Jewish people, they give the name that neighborhood Baron Hirsch.
Well, like any other synagogue, very beautiful synagogue with [INAUDIBLE] at the end of that and with a chair.
So it really was a beautiful synagogue.
Yeah, Sephardic only.
Yeah.
Not my grandparents.
My grandparents, they send me the Italian consulate.
Because my mother was with five kids.
And she didn't have the-- the-- she didn't have the-- the way how to support us.
And the Italian consulate, which they help us very-- too much.
Not only us, to many Jewish people I can tell you that.
After when-- even when the Germans was in Thessaloniki, the Italian consulate was giving some false certificates to get them out from the ghetto, which it happened to many people that I know.
So they sent me to Italy.
They proposed to my mother to send me to Italy to study over there.
Because I was the oldest of the family.
And she couldn't afford to feed us.
I was 13.
I went right to Milan to a private college, Milan, which I didn't have to pay nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were with about 300 people, 300 [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] young people with a church in it.
And I was the only Jewish at the church.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was a little behind from my class.
And they priest every night, he would take me to his office to give me some lessons to be able to be in the same level with the other students.
But I don't think this was a reason to help me out.
Because after the priest was talking to me all the time about Christianity, Jesus, et cetera, which [INAUDIBLE] was too long-- too young.
He couldn't convert me.
He's, like, I'm telling all the time to everybody that I'm not religious.
Because they made me that way.
But I'm Jewish.
In Italy?
Italian.
Well, no.
Because I was since Saloniki I was going to school, in Italian school.
And I learned Italian at school.
When I went in Italy, then I learn it better.
We speaking the Ladino.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because my mother spoke a little bit Greek, my father a little better, my grandparents maybe not at all.
They were speaking Turkey, because it was-- Macedonian was a [INAUDIBLE] from the Turkish empire at the time.
So, but we spoke Greek and Ladino.
Well, now I speak-- I hope, the English.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I speak the Ladino, the Spanish which I learned here from the Mexican people, the-- the Castilian Spanish.
That makes three.
I still speak French, which I-- the most I forget it, Germans, which I don't want to know.
In the camp, I used to speak.
But I don't want to know.
And I don't know nothing about it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And, of course, well, I can not say that I was speaking Poland or Russian, no, a little bit of those languages.
But the best language that I know is Greek, Spanish, Italian.
I was studying over there to be a mechanical engineer.
Right in the college.
It was an internal college.
No, no, no, no.
Just a dormitory-- dormitory.
And we were studying four hours a day, and four hours-- four hours theory and four hours in-- in the shop, I don't know.
Only by letters.
Oh, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Milan, I was maybe around three years and a half.
Maybe one more year I was in Rome.
No.
Around 1935-- my father dies in '34. '35, they send me to Italy.
And then when they start their anti-Semitism in Italy, which was not like in Germany-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in 1939, they send me back.
Nah, not too much, really.
Not too much.
Because I was in college with my friends studying music and everything else.
But I did not know nothing about politics.
Did my experience a what?
I did know nothing about it, because I was internally in the college.
I didn't know what was going on outside of the college.
I didn't know nothing about it.
And I was-- I didn't know how old I was at that time.
I was maybe around 19 years old.
No, I don't.
I don't remember what month.
Well, I was very happy to be with my family again.
But I couldn't help my mother or my family, because I didn't know nothing what to do, what to work with.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The [INAUDIBLE] was my brother, Shlomo, which was helping the family with a barbershop.
He became a barber, too.
That's the only income we had.
And he was taking care of the family.
I couldn't do nothing.
I was go-- I would like very much to work over there to help my mother.
But I couldn't find any work.
Because after a year, they, the Germans, came in and it was everything upside down then.
It was 1941, I guess.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 1941, '40, '41, something like that.
I don't remember exactly.
Well, Carol, this is another story.
This is another story.

When the Italian declare war against Greece, I remember one morning very early in the morning the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some policemens came to the house, a couple of policemens [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] with a-- not with a uniform.
And they took me away from my home, only me.
Because was one year that I came from Italy.
And, of course, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took me as enemy of Greece or to be sure.
Because I was young.
Maybe I was a kind of-- espionage, what do they call them?
Spy.
Spy.
